terms.definition: The principle that individuals and institutions in positions of power must justify their actions and decisions to the public and can be held responsible for outcomes.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Latin accomptare (to account), from ad- + computare (to calculate)
terms.example_usage: Ministers are held to account through parliamentary questions, demonstrating accountability to the public.
terms.common_errors: Confusing accountability with responsibility; accountability involves answering to others, responsibility is about having a duty.

terms.definition: The act of publicly supporting, recommending, or arguing in favour of a particular cause, policy, or group of people.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Latin advocatus (one called to aid), from ad- + vocare (to call)
terms.example_usage: The charity used advocacy for children's rights to push for changes in school meals policy.
terms.common_errors: Confusing advocacy with protest; advocacy can include constructive dialogue and working within existing systems.

terms.definition: The feeling of being accepted and included within a community, group, or society, which contributes to identity and social cohesion.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Middle English belongen (to go along with, be suitable)
terms.example_usage: A sense of belonging to the local community was strengthened through shared cultural events.
terms.common_errors: Assuming belonging requires uniformity; diverse communities can foster strong belonging through shared values.

terms.definition: In citizenship, a plan for government or personal income and expenditure, including the annual government Budget setting tax and spending plans.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Old French bougette (small leather bag), diminutive of bouge (bag)
terms.example_usage: The Chancellor presents the Budget to Parliament, outlining planned spending and taxation for the year.
terms.common_errors: Thinking a budget only limits spending; it is a planning tool that also projects income and priorities.

terms.definition: An organised course of action designed to achieve a particular political, social, or economic goal, often involving public communication and mobilisation.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: French campagne (open country, campaign), from Latin campus (field)
terms.example_usage: The campaign to lower the voting age involved petitions, social media, and lobbying MPs.
terms.common_errors: Limiting campaigns to elections; campaigns can target any social or political change.

terms.definition: A legally recognised member of a state or country, entitled to its rights and expected to fulfil certain duties and responsibilities.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Anglo-French citezein, from Latin civitas (city, citizenship)
terms.example_usage: As a British citizen, she had the right to vote and the responsibility to obey the law.
terms.common_errors: Conflating citizen with resident; not all residents are citizens, and citizenship confers specific legal rights.

terms.definition: Fundamental freedoms protecting individuals from government overreach, such as freedom of speech, assembly, and religion.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Latin civilis (relating to citizens) + libertas (freedom)
terms.example_usage: Civil liberties protect the right to protest peacefully without fear of arrest.
terms.common_errors: Using civil liberties and human rights interchangeably; civil liberties specifically concern freedoms from state interference.

terms.definition: The network of voluntary organisations, charities, community groups, and institutions that operate independently of government and business to serve public interests.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Latin civilis (relating to citizens) + societas (fellowship)
terms.example_usage: Civil society organisations such as charities and trade unions help hold government to account.
terms.common_errors: Thinking civil society is anti-government; it works alongside and sometimes in partnership with government.

terms.definition: A group of people living in the same area or sharing common interests, values, or characteristics, bound by mutual connection.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Latin communitas (fellowship), from communis (common, shared)
terms.example_usage: The local community organised a neighbourhood clean-up to improve their shared environment.
terms.common_errors: Assuming community means only geographical proximity; online and interest-based communities are equally valid.

terms.definition: Interest calculated on both the initial principal and accumulated interest from previous periods, causing savings or debts to grow exponentially.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Latin componere (to put together) + interesse (to be between, matter)
terms.example_usage: Compound interest meant her savings grew faster each year because interest was earned on previous interest.
terms.common_errors: Confusing with simple interest; compound interest earns interest on interest, simple interest only on the original amount.

terms.definition: A defined geographical area whose residents elect a single Member of Parliament to represent them in the House of Commons.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Latin constituere (to set up, establish)
terms.example_usage: There are 650 constituencies in the UK, each represented by one MP in Parliament.
terms.common_errors: Confusing constituency with council ward; constituencies elect MPs, wards elect local councillors.

terms.definition: The fundamental principles and established precedents according to which a state is governed, defining government powers and citizens' rights.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Latin constitutio (arrangement, system), from constituere (to establish)
terms.example_usage: The UK has an uncodified constitution made up of statutes, conventions, and common law.
terms.common_errors: Assuming the UK has no constitution because it is unwritten; it exists but is not in a single document.

terms.definition: A system of government where a monarch serves as ceremonial head of state but real political power is exercised by elected representatives within constitutional limits.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Latin constitutio (arrangement) + Greek monarkhia (rule of one)
terms.example_usage: The UK is a constitutional monarchy where the King performs ceremonial duties but Parliament makes laws.
terms.common_errors: Thinking the monarch has significant political power; the monarch acts on ministerial advice.

terms.definition: An unwritten rule or established practice followed by custom rather than being legally enforceable, such as ministerial responsibility.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Latin conventio (agreement), from con- + venire (to come)
terms.example_usage: By convention, the Prime Minister is the leader of the party that wins the most seats.
terms.common_errors: Confusing conventions with laws; conventions can be broken without legal penalty, though with political consequences.

terms.definition: A judicial institution where legal cases are heard and judged according to the law, forming part of the judiciary branch of government.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Old French cort, from Latin cohors (enclosed yard, retinue)
terms.example_usage: The Supreme Court ruled that the government's decision was unlawful.
terms.common_errors: Thinking courts only deal with criminals; civil courts handle disputes between individuals and organisations.

terms.definition: The ability to borrow money or access goods and services with the agreement to pay later, often with interest charged.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Latin creditum (a loan), from credere (to believe, trust)
terms.example_usage: Using a credit card responsibly can build a good credit score for future borrowing.
terms.common_errors: Viewing credit as free money; credit must be repaid with interest, and misuse leads to debt problems.

terms.definition: The shared beliefs, values, traditions, customs, language, and practices of a group that shape their way of life and identity.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Latin cultura (cultivation), from colere (to till, cultivate)
terms.example_usage: British culture reflects the influences of many different ethnic and religious communities.
terms.common_errors: Treating culture as static; cultures evolve through interaction, migration, and social change.

terms.definition: An amount of money owed by one party to another, typically resulting from borrowing, which usually must be repaid with interest.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Old French dette, from Latin debitum (thing owed), from debere (to owe)
terms.example_usage: Managing debt responsibly means only borrowing what you can afford to repay.
terms.common_errors: Assuming all debt is bad; mortgages and student loans can be beneficial when managed responsibly.

terms.definition: A system of government in which power is held by the people, exercised either directly or through elected representatives.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Greek demokratia, from demos (people) + kratos (power, rule)
terms.example_usage: Democracy in the UK is representative: citizens elect MPs who pass laws on their behalf.
terms.common_errors: Equating democracy with majority rule only; democracies also protect minority rights through rule of law.

terms.definition: The transfer of certain powers from central government at Westminster to regional governments in Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Latin devolutio (rolling down), from de- (down) + volvere (to roll)
terms.example_usage: Devolution gave the Scottish Parliament power to set its own income tax rates.
terms.common_errors: Confusing devolution with independence; devolved powers are granted by Westminster and could be reclaimed.

terms.definition: The unjust or prejudicial treatment of people based on characteristics such as race, gender, age, disability, or religion.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Latin discriminare (to separate, distinguish)
terms.example_usage: The Equality Act 2010 makes it unlawful to discriminate in employment because of gender.
terms.common_errors: Limiting discrimination to intentional acts; indirect discrimination through policies that disproportionately affect a group is equally unlawful.

terms.definition: The presence of a range of different characteristics, backgrounds, and perspectives within a community, including differences in ethnicity, religion, and culture.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Latin diversitas (difference, variety), from diversus (turned different ways)
terms.example_usage: The diversity of modern Britain is reflected in its many languages, religions, and cultural traditions.
terms.common_errors: Treating diversity as only about race; it encompasses gender, age, disability, religion, and socioeconomic background.

terms.definition: A formal process by which citizens vote to choose representatives to serve in government, such as general elections or local elections.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Latin electio (choice), from eligere (to pick out)
terms.example_usage: A general election is held at least every five years to elect all 650 Members of Parliament.
terms.common_errors: Thinking elections only occur at general election time; local and devolved elections also take place.

terms.definition: Active participation and involvement in political, civic, or community life, from voting and campaigning to volunteering.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: French engager (to pledge), from en- + gage (pledge)
terms.example_usage: Youth engagement in politics increased when schools introduced citizenship projects linked to local issues.
terms.common_errors: Reducing civic engagement to just voting; it includes volunteering, petitioning, campaigning, and community action.

terms.definition: A legal right to receive something, such as government benefits, public services, or protections guaranteed by law.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Anglo-French entitler (to give a title to), from Latin titulus (title)
terms.example_usage: All UK residents have an entitlement to free healthcare through the NHS.
terms.common_errors: Confusing entitlement with privilege; entitlements are legal rights, privileges are advantages not available to all.
terms.related_everyday_word: what you have a right to

terms.definition: The principle that all people should have the same rights, status, and opportunities regardless of personal characteristics, protected by the Equality Act 2010.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Latin aequalitas (evenness), from aequalis (equal)
terms.example_usage: Equality legislation ensures employers cannot pay women less than men for doing the same work.
terms.common_errors: Confusing equality with equity; equality means same treatment for all, equity means fair treatment adjusted to needs.

terms.definition: A shared cultural identity based on common ancestry, language, traditions, and customs that distinguishes one group from another.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Greek ethnikos (national), from ethnos (people, nation)
terms.example_usage: The UK census collects data on ethnicity to understand the diversity of the population.
terms.common_errors: Confusing ethnicity with race; ethnicity is about shared cultural identity, race is a social construct based on physical characteristics.

terms.definition: The branch of government responsible for implementing and enforcing laws, led by the Prime Minister and Cabinet in the UK.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Latin executivus (carrying out), from exsequi (to follow out)
terms.example_usage: The executive proposes new laws and implements policies, while Parliament scrutinises them.
terms.common_errors: Confusing the executive with Parliament; the executive is the PM and ministers who run departments.
terms.related_everyday_word: the people who run the government

terms.definition: The total amount of money spent by a government, organisation, or individual over a period, including on goods, services, and programmes.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Latin expendere (to weigh out, pay out), from ex- + pendere (to weigh)
terms.example_usage: Government expenditure on the NHS accounts for a significant proportion of public spending.
terms.common_errors: Using expenditure and spending as if they differ; they are synonymous, with expenditure being the formal term.

terms.definition: A service offered by banks or providers to help people manage, save, borrow, or invest money, such as savings accounts, loans, and insurance.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Latin finis (settlement of debt) + productum (thing produced)
terms.example_usage: Before choosing a financial product like a credit card, consumers should compare interest rates.
terms.common_errors: Thinking financial products are only for wealthy adults; savings accounts and budgeting apps are relevant to young people.

terms.definition: The electoral system used for UK general elections where the candidate with the most votes in each constituency wins, regardless of majority.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Horse racing metaphor: the first to pass the finishing post wins
terms.example_usage: Under first-past-the-post, a candidate can win with 35% of the vote if no other candidate gets more.
terms.common_errors: Assuming the winner always has majority support; a candidate can win with a small plurality.

terms.definition: In citizenship, the right to vote in elections, historically expanded through campaigns for universal suffrage.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Old French franchise (freedom), from franc (free)
terms.example_usage: The franchise was extended to all men over 21 in 1918 and to women on equal terms in 1928.
terms.common_errors: Confusing with the business meaning (licensed brand); in citizenship, franchise means the right to vote.

terms.definition: The state of being free from oppressive restrictions, with the ability to act, speak, and think as one chooses within legal limits.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Old English freodom, from freo (free) + -dom (state of being)
terms.example_usage: Freedom of expression allows people to voice opinions but does not protect speech that incites hatred.
terms.common_errors: Treating freedom as absolute; all freedoms have legal limits to protect others and public order.

terms.definition: The group of people who have authority to govern a country, make laws, and direct public policy, led in the UK by the PM and Cabinet.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Latin gubernare (to steer, govern), from Greek kybernan
terms.example_usage: The government introduced legislation to address climate change by reducing carbon emissions.
terms.common_errors: Confusing government with Parliament; government is the PM and ministers, Parliament includes all MPs and Lords.

terms.definition: The traditions, monuments, and cultural achievements passed down from previous generations and valued as part of a community's identity.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Old French heritage (inheritance), from heriter, from Latin hereditas
terms.example_usage: Britain's heritage includes historic buildings, parliamentary traditions, and diverse cultural contributions.
terms.common_errors: Limiting heritage to physical monuments; it includes language, customs, and shared memories.

terms.definition: Fundamental rights and freedoms belonging to every person regardless of nationality, sex, ethnicity, or religion, set out in international law.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Latin humanus (human) + Old French droit (right)
terms.example_usage: The Universal Declaration of Human Rights establishes rights to life, liberty, and security.
terms.common_errors: Thinking human rights only apply in extreme situations; they apply to everyday life including education and privacy.

terms.definition: The Human Rights Act 1998, UK legislation incorporating the European Convention on Human Rights into domestic law, allowing rights enforcement in UK courts.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Named after the UK legislation passed in 1998
terms.example_usage: The Human Rights Act allows individuals to challenge government actions that violate their rights in British courts.
terms.common_errors: Confusing the HRA with the ECHR; the HRA brings ECHR rights into UK law for local enforcement.

terms.definition: The characteristics, beliefs, values, and sense of self that define who a person is, shaped by nationality, ethnicity, religion, and experience.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Latin identitas (sameness), from idem (the same)
terms.example_usage: A person's identity may be shaped by their nationality, religious beliefs, and local community.
terms.common_errors: Treating identity as fixed; identity is dynamic and can evolve through experience and interaction.

terms.definition: The practice of ensuring all individuals, regardless of background, can fully participate in and benefit from community and civic life.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Latin inclusio (shutting in); modern sense: bringing in
terms.example_usage: The council promoted inclusion by providing wheelchair access and translation services at public meetings.
terms.common_errors: Confusing inclusion with tolerance; inclusion requires active effort to remove barriers, not just passive acceptance.

terms.definition: Money received regularly from employment, investments, pensions, or state benefits that a person uses to meet their needs.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Old English in + cuman (to come in)
terms.example_usage: A household's total income includes wages, benefits, and interest earned on savings.
terms.common_errors: Confusing gross income with net income; gross is before tax, net is what you actually receive.

terms.definition: A financial product where a person pays regular premiums in exchange for protection against potential financial losses from specified risks.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Old French enseurer (to make sure)
terms.example_usage: Car insurance is a legal requirement in the UK, protecting drivers against accident costs.
terms.common_errors: Assuming insurance covers everything; policies have exclusions and conditions that limit coverage.

terms.definition: The charge for borrowing money or the payment received for saving it, expressed as a percentage of the amount over time.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Latin interesse (to be between, to matter)
terms.example_usage: The bank paid 3% interest on savings, meaning one hundred pounds earned three pounds per year.
terms.common_errors: Not distinguishing between interest earned on savings and interest charged on borrowing.
terms.related_everyday_word: what you earn or pay on money

terms.definition: Putting money into financial products, property, or businesses with the expectation of generating a return or profit over time.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Latin investire (to clothe, to surround); extended to mean committing money
terms.example_usage: Investing in stocks carries more risk than a savings account but may yield higher returns.
terms.common_errors: Assuming investments always make money; values can go down as well as up.
terms.related_everyday_word: putting money in to get more back

terms.definition: Relating to the courts and judges, or the branch of government responsible for interpreting the law independently of the executive and legislature.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Latin judicialis (of a court of justice), from judex (judge)
terms.example_usage: Judicial review allows courts to check whether government decisions are lawful and fair.
terms.common_errors: Confusing judicial with legislative; judicial interprets law, legislative makes it.

terms.definition: The branch of government comprising the system of courts and judges responsible for interpreting and applying the law independently.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Latin judiciarius (of the courts), from judicium (judgement)
terms.example_usage: The independence of the judiciary means judges cannot be directed by ministers on decisions.
terms.common_errors: Confusing the judiciary with the legal profession generally; the judiciary specifically means judges and courts.

terms.definition: The branch of government responsible for debating, amending, and passing laws; in the UK, the House of Commons, House of Lords, and the Monarch.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Latin legislatura, from lex (law) + lator (proposer)
terms.example_usage: Bills must pass through both houses of the legislature before receiving Royal Assent.
terms.common_errors: Confusing legislature with government; the legislature makes laws, the government implements them.

terms.definition: Personal freedoms and rights that citizens possess, particularly freedom from excessive government interference in their lives.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Latin libertas (freedom), from liber (free)
terms.example_usage: Surveillance cameras raised concerns about the balance between security and individual liberties.
terms.common_errors: Using liberties and rights interchangeably; liberties emphasise freedom from interference, rights emphasise entitlements.

terms.definition: To seek to influence a politician or institution on a particular issue, or the group of people engaged in such influence.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Medieval Latin lobia (covered walkway); lobbyists waited in Parliament's lobby to speak with MPs
terms.example_usage: Environmental groups lobbied MPs to vote against the proposed motorway expansion.
terms.common_errors: Assuming all lobbying is corrupt; it is a legitimate democratic activity when conducted transparently.

terms.definition: The authority granted to a government by voters through an election to carry out the policies in their manifesto.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Latin mandatum (command), from manus (hand) + dare (to give)
terms.example_usage: The party claimed a mandate to implement education reforms after winning a large majority.
terms.common_errors: Assuming a mandate gives unlimited power; it is limited to the policies presented to voters.

terms.definition: A public declaration of the policies, aims, and values that a political party promises to implement if elected to government.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Italian manifesto (public declaration), from Latin manifestus (obvious)
terms.example_usage: Each party published a manifesto outlining plans for healthcare, education, and taxation.
terms.common_errors: Treating manifesto promises as legally binding; they are political commitments, not enforceable contracts.

terms.definition: A long-term loan secured against a property, used to purchase a home, repaid with interest over typically 25 to 30 years.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Old French mort gage (dead pledge); the pledge dies when debt is paid or property seized
terms.example_usage: Taking out a mortgage allowed the family to buy their first home, repaying over 25 years.
terms.common_errors: Not understanding that the home can be repossessed if mortgage payments are not maintained.

terms.definition: A compulsory UK tax deducted from earnings to fund state benefits including the state pension, NHS, and unemployment support.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: National + Insurance; established by the National Insurance Act 1911
terms.example_usage: National insurance contributions are deducted from wages and help fund the state pension and NHS.
terms.common_errors: Thinking NI is a separate savings pot for each person; it funds current spending, not individual accounts.
terms.related_everyday_word: the tax for benefits and NHS

terms.definition: The legal status of belonging to a particular country, determining rights such as the right to live, work, and vote there.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Latin natio (birth, people) + -alis (pertaining to)
terms.example_usage: British nationality entitles a person to a UK passport and the right to vote.
terms.common_errors: Confusing nationality with ethnicity; nationality is a legal status, ethnicity is a cultural identity.

terms.definition: The largest political party not in government, whose role is to scrutinise and challenge the government's policies and decisions.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Latin oppositio (setting against), from ob- + ponere (to place)
terms.example_usage: The Leader of the Opposition challenged the PM on NHS waiting times during PMQs.
terms.common_errors: Seeing opposition as purely obstructive; it performs a vital democratic function of holding government to account.

terms.definition: The supreme legislative body of the UK, consisting of the House of Commons, House of Lords, and the Monarch, responsible for making laws.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Old French parlement (speaking, discussion), from parler (to speak)
terms.example_usage: Parliament debates proposed laws and holds the government to account through questions and committees.
terms.common_errors: Using Parliament and government interchangeably; Parliament passes laws, government proposes and implements them.

terms.definition: Active involvement in democratic and civic processes, including voting, campaigning, volunteering, and engaging with decision-making.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Latin participatio (sharing), from pars (part) + capere (to take)
terms.example_usage: Youth participation in council consultations helped shape the design of the new community park.
terms.common_errors: Limiting participation to voting; it includes volunteering, writing to MPs, and community action.

terms.definition: A regular payment made after retirement, funded by the state through national insurance or through private workplace and personal schemes.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Latin pensio (payment), from pendere (to pay, weigh)
terms.example_usage: Workers are automatically enrolled into a workplace pension to save for retirement.
terms.common_errors: Assuming the state pension alone is sufficient; additional private pension saving is consistently recommended.

terms.definition: A formal written request signed by many people, asking an authority such as Parliament to take action on a particular issue.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Latin petitio (a request), from petere (to seek, ask)
terms.example_usage: A petition with 100,000 signatures triggers a debate in Parliament on the issue raised.
terms.common_errors: Assuming a petition guarantees action; it raises awareness but does not compel a specific response.

terms.definition: An individual's belief that their political participation can make a difference and influence government decisions.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Greek politikos (of citizens) + Latin efficacia (effectiveness)
terms.example_usage: High political efficacy encourages people to vote because they believe their voice matters.
terms.common_errors: Confusing with political knowledge; efficacy is about belief in making a difference, not knowledge of politics.

terms.definition: An organised group with shared political beliefs who seek to influence government by winning elections and forming government.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Greek politikos (of citizens) + Old French partie (side)
terms.example_usage: The main UK political parties include the Conservatives, Labour, and the Liberal Democrats.
terms.common_errors: Confusing political parties with pressure groups; parties seek government, pressure groups seek influence from outside.

terms.definition: A preconceived negative opinion towards a person or group based on stereotypes rather than experience or evidence.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Latin praejudicium (prior judgement), from prae- + judicium (judgement)
terms.example_usage: Prejudice based on religion can lead to discrimination and social exclusion.
terms.common_errors: Confusing prejudice with discrimination; prejudice is the attitude, discrimination is the action.

terms.definition: An electoral system where seats won are roughly proportional to the percentage of votes received, producing a more representative Parliament.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Latin proportio (comparative relation) + repraesentare (to make present)
terms.example_usage: Under PR, a party winning 30% of votes would receive approximately 30% of seats.
terms.common_errors: Thinking PR is one system; there are many variants including party list, STV, and mixed-member.

terms.definition: A direct vote by the electorate on a single political question, such as the 2016 EU membership referendum.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Latin referendum (thing to be referred), from referre (to carry back)
terms.example_usage: The 2016 referendum asked voters whether the UK should remain in or leave the European Union.
terms.common_errors: Assuming UK referendums are legally binding; they are advisory, though politically hard to ignore.

terms.definition: An organised system of beliefs, practices, and worship relating to a divine power, providing moral guidance and community.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Latin religio (reverence, obligation), possibly from religare (to bind)
terms.example_usage: Freedom of religion means people can practise any faith or choose to have none.
terms.common_errors: Treating religion as only personal belief; it also has communal, cultural, and social dimensions.

terms.definition: The principle that elected officials act on behalf of citizens, or the extent to which diverse groups are reflected in positions of power.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Latin repraesentare (to make present)
terms.example_usage: Calls for better representation led to efforts to increase women and ethnic minority MPs.
terms.common_errors: Confusing with delegate model; representatives may use their own judgement rather than following constituents.

terms.definition: A person elected to act and make decisions on behalf of a larger group, such as an MP representing their constituency.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Latin repraesentare (to make present)
terms.example_usage: The MP acted as a representative, raising local hospital closure concerns in Parliament.
terms.common_errors: Assuming representatives must always vote as constituents wish; the trustee model allows independent judgement.

terms.definition: A citizenship value involving treating others with dignity, recognising their rights, and valuing diverse perspectives.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Latin respectus (regard), from re- + specere (to look)
terms.example_usage: Respect for different cultures and beliefs is essential for a cohesive society.
terms.common_errors: Confusing respect with agreement; respecting someone's right to a view does not mean agreeing with it.

terms.definition: Legal entitlements and moral claims that every person possesses, including civil, political, economic, and social rights protected by law.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Old English riht (just, proper), related to Latin rectus (right, straight)
terms.example_usage: The right to a fair trial means everyone is presumed innocent until proven guilty.
terms.common_errors: Thinking rights are unlimited; rights can be limited to protect others or national security.

terms.definition: The principle that everyone, including the government, is subject to the law, which must be applied equally and fairly.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Old English regol (rule) + lagu (law)
terms.example_usage: The rule of law means even the Prime Minister can be taken to court for acting unlawfully.
terms.common_errors: Confusing rule of law with rule by law; rule of law constrains government, rule by law is government using law as a tool.

terms.definition: Money set aside from income for future use, typically held in bank accounts where it may earn interest.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Old French sauver (to save), from Latin salvare (to make safe)
terms.example_usage: Building up savings in an ISA provides a financial safety net for unexpected expenses.
terms.common_errors: Assuming all savings accounts are the same; interest rates, access, and tax treatment vary significantly.

terms.definition: The constitutional principle that government power should be divided among three independent branches to prevent abuse of power.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Latin separatio (division) + Old French poer (power)
terms.example_usage: Separation of powers means Parliament makes laws, government implements them, and courts interpret them.
terms.common_errors: Assuming the UK has strict separation; in practice there is overlap, as ministers sit in Parliament.

terms.definition: The supreme authority of a state to govern itself, or the ultimate source of political power within a country.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Old French souverainete, from souverain (supreme), from Latin super (above)
terms.example_usage: Parliamentary sovereignty means Parliament is the supreme legal authority and can make or repeal any law.
terms.common_errors: Confusing parliamentary with popular sovereignty; in the UK, legal sovereignty rests with Parliament.

terms.definition: A law formally enacted by Parliament through the legislative process, also known as an Act of Parliament.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Latin statutum (a decree), from statuere (to set up)
terms.example_usage: The Equality Act 2010 is a statute that protects people from discrimination.
terms.common_errors: Confusing statute law with common law; statute is made by Parliament, common law develops through court decisions.

terms.definition: The right to vote in political elections, historically gained through campaigns to extend voting rights to women and the working class.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Latin suffragium (vote, right to vote)
terms.example_usage: The suffragette movement campaigned for women's suffrage, achieving equal voting rights in 1928.
terms.common_errors: Confusing suffrage with suffragettes; suffrage means the right to vote, suffragettes campaigned for it.

terms.definition: A compulsory financial contribution levied by the government on income, purchases, or property to fund public services.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Latin taxare (to evaluate, assess)
terms.example_usage: Income tax helps fund public services such as schools, hospitals, and roads.
terms.common_errors: Assuming tax is only income tax; taxes include VAT, council tax, corporation tax, and many others.
terms.related_everyday_word: what you pay to the government

terms.definition: The willingness to accept and respect behaviour, beliefs, and practices different from one's own, even where there is disagreement.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Latin tolerantia (endurance), from tolerare (to bear)
terms.example_usage: Tolerance of religious diversity means accepting people's right to practise their faith.
terms.common_errors: Treating tolerance as enthusiastic approval; tolerance means accepting difference alongside personal disagreement.

terms.definition: A person who freely offers their time, skills, or labour for a community or charitable purpose without payment.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: French volontaire (willing), from Latin voluntarius (of free will)
terms.example_usage: Volunteers staffed the local food bank, distributing meals to families in need.
terms.common_errors: Undervaluing volunteering as less important than paid work; it is essential to civil society.

terms.definition: A formal expression of choice in an election or decision, or the act of casting such a choice, which is a fundamental democratic right.
terms.subject: Citizenship
terms.etymology: Latin votum (a vow, wish), from vovere (to vow)
terms.example_usage: Every citizen aged 18 or over has the right to cast a vote in a UK general election.
terms.common_errors: Assuming not voting has no impact; low turnout weakens democratic legitimacy.
